frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: observer combines prior with noisy likelihood to produce posterior estimates

frameworks.name: Weber's law
frameworks.one_line: classic psychophysical principle that response variability scales with magnitude

frameworks.name: Fechnerian logarithmic encoding
frameworks.one_line: internal representation of magnitude as logarithm of stimulus intensity

core_question: Does Bayesian estimation predict Weber-like variability, or can it produce the opposite pattern under skewed priors and rewards?
key_assumptions: observer combines prior with likelihood via Bayes rule
magnitudes are internally encoded on a logarithmic scale
reward function shapes the estimator's loss
numerosity estimation indexes general magnitude perception
manipulations: prior distribution skewness over numerosities (small- vs large-frequent)
reward function across blocks (rewarding small vs large magnitudes)
key_findings: Bayesian observer exhibits anti-Weber behavior when large magnitudes are more frequent or rewarding
human subjects show the same anti-Weber pattern, breaking the classic psychophysical result
subject responses are best reproduced by a logarithmic encoding of magnitudes
interpretations: increasing variability with magnitude may reflect skewness of natural priors rather than encoding noise
Fechner logarithmic encoding and anti-Weber behavior can coexist within a Bayesian framework
